zotero.org/google-docs/?RBeQSO). Image
quality control was performed by CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157)
[^67^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6NA1Xm).

### ***Cell phenotyping***

For the TMAs' cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either
cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were
clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers
(Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the
R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and
cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as
cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly
performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the
detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed
for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the
0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel\'s signal
was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by
marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized
map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM
node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S. Fig. 1c**) by
using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
e label scores according to the scaled
expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (**S. Fig. 1c**) by
using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed
firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune
cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (**S. Fig. 1c**). All the
cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell
population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion
for each patient's sample.

For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using
unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367)
[^18^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?ohnqa1). Outliers were
removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity,
normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5
[^68^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Td9ajN), and scaled by
Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of
*sigma=1*, *learning_rate=1*, *clustering_threshold=100*,
*undefined_threshold=0.0005*, *other_threshold=0.4*. A total of 6 cell
types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S. Table 13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+
T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table
(**S. Table 13**) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are
images S100_iOme and S110_iOme, which were manually gated with scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) [^69^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?0eresq) due
to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our
annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by
expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)
staining, showing agreement across samples (**S. Fig. 4l**). The MHCII+
cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the
annotations to the t-CycIF images.

### **Cancer cell phenotype clustering**

Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed
outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method
section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based
on the expression levels of the markers: \"Ki67\", \"Vimentin\",
\"pSTAT1\", \"MHC-I\", \"ECadherin\", \"yH2AX\", \"pTBK1\", \"cPARP1\",
\"CK7\", \"PDL1_488\", and morphological features: \"Area\",
\"Eccentricity\", and \"Roundness\" into a 2500-node self-organized map
(SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were
clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954).

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
" into a 2500-node self-organized map
(SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were
clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954).
For this, the number of clusters (*k*) was assessed from 2 to 12. For
each value of *k* assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of
the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and
morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm
"partitioning around medoids" (pam) was used to cluster the selected
dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best
number of cancer meta-clusters, *k*, by assessing the resultant
consensus-clustering matrix for each value of *k* and its cumulative
density distribution (**Ext. material - Fig. 3**). The cancer
meta-clusters were named according to the expression of relevant markers
in each one (**Ext. material - Fig. 2**).

### ***Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis***

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality
control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius
of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap
(RRID:SCR_024751) [^69^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?5Mos0I). We
clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate
recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k**=**40

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
.zotero.org/google-docs/?5Mos0I). We
clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate
recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k**=**40
employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package scimap. Every
cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value of k, we
used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick
[^70^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?B2IjP9) (**Ext. material -
Fig. 3**). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods
of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis
using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the
robustness of our approach (**Ext. material - Fig. 3**). The proportion
of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of
cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.

### ***Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis***

To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity
of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial
transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different
responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data,
we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341)
[^71^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JOgS7e).

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341)
[^71^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JOgS7e). For the QC, we
excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected,
more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin
genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat\'s
SCTransform function.

Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an
expert pathologist as 'Cancer', 'TSI' (tumor-stroma interface with
partial tumor and stromal morphology), 'Immune-Stromal' and 'other'.
Spots annotated as 'other' have been excluded from the analysis. Within
'Immune-Stromal' spots, we have distinguished an additional type -
'TSI2', defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to
either 'Cancer' or 'TSI' spots. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) ^44^. The MHCII
signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA,
HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA,
HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed
MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the
median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
pared the distribution of the computed
MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the
median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to
chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor
response was adopted from Böhm et al, 2015
[^72^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?JpjFmE). An IFN-γ signature,
obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene
set collection (RRID:SCR_016863)
[^73^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v1TFTd), was also quantified
per spot. Statistical significance has been computed using a two-sided
Wilcoxon Rank Sum test.

### ***Machine learning supervised classification***

The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had
the following pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein
marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score);
(2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness),
scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the proportion of
neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type;
additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like
short- or long-OS the model included patients' clinical information --
FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker
and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ed patients' clinical information --
FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker
and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range
were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than
100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent
machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling
was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20%
into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.

To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm
for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the
PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695,
[[https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret]{.underline}](https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret)).
We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of:
BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected
Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance
(weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (**S. Table
14**). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to determine the
number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
diction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S. Table 15**).

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python
scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577)
[^74^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?XlLOJH) for binary
classification of cells into clinicomolecular groups
([https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA]{.underline}). The models\'
training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop
iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using
the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 30 data
partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model
robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve
similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based
on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (**S. Fig.
3n**). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a
Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model
(*min_samples_split*, *min_samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
sed to tune the hyperparameters of the model
(*min_samples_split*, *min_samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of
hyperparameters' values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean
weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each
iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three
independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a
cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on
classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting
the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target
variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we
evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set
partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell
level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard
deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported.
Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells,
Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified
the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of
overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
urvival prediction models, we stratified
the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of
overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below
the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

### ***Deconvoluted RNA-expression and CIBERT sort profile***

For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and
MHCI), we used publicly available RNA-expression data from The Cancer
Genome Atlas (TCGA) (RRID:SCR_003193) ^5^. The deconvoluted
cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with PRISM and adopted from
the previous work ^26^. The inferred cell proportions by CIBERSORT were
obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository
[^75^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?C7Mdte).

### ***RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment***

For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read
counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463)
[^76^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?RgUjj2) from the Genomics
Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also
obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository
[^75^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?I1JWZK). We stratified the
tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1
in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
/google-docs/?I1JWZK). We stratified the
tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1
in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We
assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group
with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33)
expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with
less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used
the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) ^31^ for count normalization and
differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change
\>=2, FDR \< 1\^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or
sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or
sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal PantherDB
v19.0 ^32^, we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the
Reactome database (RRID:SCR_003485).

For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=202), we
first stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups according to
the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
t. We
assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest
(below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66)
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression
analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed
groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the
difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest
and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with
differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold
change \>=1 or Log2-fold change \<=1 and FDR\<1\^-3. Overexpressed genes
are reported in **S. Table 10**.

### ***scRNA-seq analysis***

We retrieved scRNA-seq gene count matrices from 51 HGSC samples,
pre-processed and annotated
[^23^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?6j34Lb), from the Gene
Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples
containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis,
resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from
27 patients. We assessed the activity of MHCII signature across cell
types by computing the normalized Ucell score using the R UCell package
(RRID:SCR_027109) v1.3.1
[^44^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?HPzMBY). The MHCII signature
comprised of the following genes: *HLA-DMA*, *HLA-DMB*, *HLA-DOA*,

[^44^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?HPzMBY). The MHCII signature
comprised of the following genes: *HLA-DMA*, *HLA-DMB*, *HLA-DOA*,
*HLA-DOB*, *HLA-DPA1*, *HLA-DPB1*, *HLA-DQA1*, *HLA-DQA2*, *HLA-DQB1*,
*HLA-DRA*, *HLA-DRB1*, *HLA-DRB5*, *CD74.* Based on the MHCII Ucell

and cell type, pseudo-bulk gene expression profiles were generated using

(RRID:SCR_016341) v4.3.0
[^77^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?AWcfq3). According to the

stratified into three equal-sized groups: high, medium, and low,
according to tertile distribution (**S. Fig. 4c**). To assess the
correlation between cancer-specific MHCII expression and IFN-γ in immune
populations, pseudo-bulk expression of the *IFNG* gene was computed for
each cell type per sample. Only immune cells were included in the
correlation analysis, as they exhibited higher *IFNG* expression
compared to fibroblasts or epithelial/cancer cells (**S. Fig. 5l**).
Pearson's correlation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially
expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in

Pearson's correlation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially

analyzed with the limma package (RRID:SCR_010943). Patient information

### **GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis** 

We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors
obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024 ^16^, and is
publicly available at
[[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672).
The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the
tumor--stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and
stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were
initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and
IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the
TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data
had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed
normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) ^31^,
followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
uality control prior to analysis. We performed
normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) ^31^,
followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were
corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206).

Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic
deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene
expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
([[https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism]{.underline}](https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism)),
a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial
transcriptomics datasets
[^78^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?pjFFQe). This method not only
estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles
for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw
scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577,
along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we
downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final
dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in
resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were
grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T
cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B
cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ories. The final reference included: 9,168 T
cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B
cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90
endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late
erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient.
Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with
low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both
'cell types' and 'cell states'.

We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA)
scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific
deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess
the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we
correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from
BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y \~
exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating 'Patient' as a covariate (y
\~ exp(x) \| Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the
same tumor.

### ***Ligand-receptor analysis***

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing

r.

### ***Ligand-receptor analysis***

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing
them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq
analysis). MultiNicheNetR
[^79^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?MdBFlQ)
([[https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git]{.underline}](https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high
and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the
analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of
five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were
required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion
of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates
were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log
fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized
ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions
enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
lue ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized
ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions
enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These
candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics
dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We
correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor
deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma
segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

### ***Immunopeptidomics***

We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC
samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to
the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the
interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more
information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was
performed as previously described
[^80^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?YdoerY) with minor
modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed
on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by
mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
ociated into a single-cell suspension by
mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected
to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer
composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors
(Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich,
I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at
500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at
25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was
applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher),
pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of
anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.
The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the
following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM
NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were
dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
tide complexes bound to the resin were
dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges
(Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in
0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were
eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1%
TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The
eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using
the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
into a single sample.

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto
an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1
min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),
with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs
cm^−2^ to 0.64 Vs cm^−2^ in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were
acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above
the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at \< 700
m/z or 3 Th \>800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of
20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy
(CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting
from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm^−2^ to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm^−2^.
Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was
used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was
evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest
standard (ThermoFischer Scientific).

Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational
suite (RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.
MSFragger [^81^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?06hrYE) was
employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a
few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and
'null', allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained
to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da,
and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da)
was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation
of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher
[^82^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?B9ESPF), applying a false
discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match
(PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out
using IonQuant [^83^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?bU4Soz),
enabling between-run normalization while disabling the
match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434)
[^84^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?BLFQMa) with default
settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434)
[^84^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?BLFQMa) with default
settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while
the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides
confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis.
Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting
from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286,
[[https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant]{.underline}](https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant))
[^85^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?w0V9Ed) with default settings
and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis,
peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor
antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets
(UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides
in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion
based on matching t-CyCIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low
(patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes
unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped
genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between
the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the
high group was performed using Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575)

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
d low groups and were set as a difference between
the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the
high group was performed using Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575)
[^86^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?wyaEfK) (GO Biological
Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms
were recorded.

### ***TCR repertoire analysis***

We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC
samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples
(total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study. Tumor samples underwent
RNA sequencing with Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using the Illumina Stranded
Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero plus), aiming at \>100 million read pairs
at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data has been processed with
Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes
2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were
extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725)
[^87^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9WV0sl), following the
RNA-seq workflow
([[https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/]{.underline}](https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/))
with default settings. We used 'mixcr analyze rnaseq', followed by
'mixcr exportQc align' and 'mixcr exportQc chainUsage' for quality
control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
s. We used 'mixcr analyze rnaseq', followed by
'mixcr exportQc align' and 'mixcr exportQc chainUsage' for quality
control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq
(non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully
aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones,
with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in
7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group).
Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly
represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal
diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R
package (RRID:SCR_023089)
[^88^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?A4XHGI). We employed the
'repExplore' function with the \"volume\" method to compare TCR clone
counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to
the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): (1) High MHCII group: 6
samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6
samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (3) Medium (the rest).
Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were
evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for
multiple comparisons using the Holm method.

### ***iPDCs flow cytometry analysis***

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
gh and low groups were
evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for
multiple comparisons using the Holm method.

### ***iPDCs flow cytometry analysis***

The dataset used for this analysis was generated in Launonen et al.,
2024 [^16^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?8SpPPv). Briefly, tumor
tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was
dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an
omentum-derived matrix
[^89^](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?v8otat). Cultures were
treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was
performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting
dataset was made publicly available at
[https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}.

In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells
(single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low
groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all
immune (CD45⁺) and myeloid cells (CD11c⁺ and CD14⁺) (**S. Fig. 6**).
MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the
bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune
and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was
quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
d the median value observed in immune
and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was
quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single
cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between
MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was
assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed
using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and
custom R scripts.

### ***Survival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival
(RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by
calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total
cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more
than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, **S. Fig. 1b**) were selected
for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell
phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune
populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core
post-preprocessing (n=244, **S. Fig. 1b**). Patient stratification based
on cell population used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above
median) and low (below median) groups.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
e
post-preprocessing (n=244, **S. Fig. 1b**). Patient stratification based
on cell population used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above
median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split
into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66,
and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same
approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular
profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only
high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.

### ***Statistical analyses***

We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The difference
in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided
Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The
difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a
two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were
assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted
P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted
P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

## **FIGURE LEGENDS**

### **Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.**

**a** Study schematics. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics
tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC
profiles: *BRCA*loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-*BRCA*wt),
homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification
(*CCNE1amp*), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md

assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).
High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract
single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline
(CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical
covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and
molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical
methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation,
both incorporating external cohorts. **b** Antibody panel employed for
t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. **c** A bar plot displaying the
composition of cancer cells, stromal cells, myeloid cells, lymphocytes,
and unassigned cells per tumor. The bar plot is sorted by cancer cell
abundance. **d** UMAP projection of the analyzed cells, the colors
correspond to their main cell type group assignment. **e** Example of
t-CycIF image. **f** Cell type annotation of the image in panel d**.**
**g** Bar plot illustrating the composition of immune cells per tumor.
The bar plot is sorted according to the CD11c+ myeloid cell proportion.
**h** UMAP projection of immune cells coloured by their annotation as in
panel f. **i** t-CycIF image displaying the functional marker Ki67; this

to their proportion of CD8.T cells neighbors, areas with higher CD8.T

cluster assignment (at the left) and by the expression of relevant

median expression of markers per cancer cell cluster. All cancer cells
detected in the study were included in the heatmap (number of tumors =

### **Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC** 

**a** A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline 'CEFIIRA'.
Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example,

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
on as a key prognostic feature in HGSC** 

**a** A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline 'CEFIIRA'.
Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example,
predicting if cancer cells belong to either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors.
Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target
(for example, *BRCA*loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation
iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their
corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the
remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to
predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to
either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are
calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested
cross-validations, the model's median accuracy and consensus critical
features are identified. **b-c** Heatmap displaying the median accuracy
(F1 score) of CEFIIRA models in predicting whether cells belong to
tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated
molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations (columns). The 95%

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.md
vector_chunks.text:
dels in predicting whether cells belong to
tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated
molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations (columns). The 95%
confidence intervals for the F1 scores are shown in parentheses. The
results corresponding to PDS samples are in panel **b,** and in panel
**c** for IDS samples. **d-e** Dot plots displaying the log2 fold change
in scaled marker intensity between cells from short- and long-survival
patients with HRP tumors. The markers displayed correspond to the top
five most important features for each model (excluding clinical
variables). Only markers that were statistically different between

unadjusted p \< 0.05). The assessment was performed for HRP tumors, the
analysis for PDS samples is shown in panel **d** and in panel **e** for
IDS samples. **f** t-CycIF images of independent samples, displaying the
expression of relevant markers. **g** Heatmap of the median accuracy

constructed and validated using PDS samples. The 95% confidence
intervals of the F1 scores are shown in parentheses. **h** Heatmap
displaying the median expression, by molecular profile, of the top five

### **Supplementary Figure 1**

**a** Assignment of the tumors into the four different molecular
profiles. **b** Flowchart of various quality control criteria used for
tumors in the multiomics cohort. Different quality control criteria were
applied for different analyses. For example, in the survival analysis of
immune cell infiltration, only tumors with matched clinical data and
more than one core per tumor (N=244) were included. Additionally, for
the survival analysis of cancer cell proportions, only tumors with more
than 5% cancer cells (N=233) were analyzed. **c** Flowchart representing
the strategy used to assign cells from t-CycIF images into different
populations according to the expression of indicated markers. **d**
Heatmap of the median expression of cell lineage markers per labeled
cell phenotypes. Median values per cell phenotype from all tumors after
the first QC (N=258) are shown in the heatmap. **e,f** Box plots of the
proportion of immune cells in tumors detected with t-CycIF, grouped by
annotations made by an expert pathologist using immunohistochemistry
(IHC) on matching tumor sections. For each tumor, the pathologist\'s
annotation reflects the number of immune cells in the core with the
highest number of IHC-positive cells (out of four cores per tumor), for
example: \<20 indicates fewer than 20 cells in the core with the most
IHC-positive cells.

population is reflected. **e** The panel displays the proportions of
CD8+ T cells and panel **f** the proportion of CD20+ lymphocytes. The
differences between groups were calculated using a two-tailed Wilcoxon
rank-sum test. The black horizontal lines represent the median, the
boxes extend from the first to the third quartile, and the whiskers
indicate the values at 1.5 times the interquartile range. Individual
dots represent the values per tumor. **g** Heatmap of the median
expression of functional markers per labeled cell phenotypes. Median
values per cell phenotype from all tumors after the first QC (N=258) are
shown in the heatmap.

2 20 Institute for Molecular Medicine Finland, Helsinki Institute of Life Sciences, University of Helsinki, Helsinki, 38 Finland. 39 21 Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology Clinical Trials Unit, Helsinki University 40 Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 41 22 Lead contact. 42 Abstract 43 The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, 44 yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly 45 understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC 46 from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. 47 Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically 48 heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the 49 tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we 50 identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. 51 Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic 52 datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune 53 activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived 54 immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with 55 increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy.

d TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived 54 immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with 55 increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers 56 novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification 57 in HGSC. 58 INTRODUCTION 59 The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, 60 immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 1. The 61 continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is 62 fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and 63 therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most 64 prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 2,3, characterized by high genomic 65 instability 4,5 and significant tumor heterogeneity 6. This heterogeneity is evident in 66 various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME 67 cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 7. The genetic 68 diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with 69 a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 70 BRCA1/2 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher 71

3 lymphocyte infiltration 8,9. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) 72 tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 10 and unique responses to 73 combinations with immunotherapies 11,12. Moreover, we and others have shown that 74 exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by 75 enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 13–15, resulting in 76 spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 16. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex 77 relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor 78 ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment 79 strategies for patients with HGSC. 80 We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single 81 cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and 82 multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and 83 mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial 84 TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that 85 associate with patient outcomes.

a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial 84 TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that 85 associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, 86 CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate 87 single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. 88 We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression 89 specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of 90 immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune 91 hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 92 clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune 93 cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for 94 targeted interventions in HGSC. 95 RESULTS 96 Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors 97 To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical 98 prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic profiling including 99 targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA 100 methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 101 advanced HGSCs (Fig. 1a).

and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA 100 methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 101 advanced HGSCs (Fig. 1a). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking 102

4 surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS 103 n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking 104 surgery, IDS n=158). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven 105 patients. Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected, 106 with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months) (S. Table 1). Most 107 patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and 108 the median progression-free survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). 109 We conducted a hierarchical multidimensional analysis to assign the tumors’ 110 molecular profiles. Firstly, BRCA1/2 germline or somatic mutation status was 111 assessed using targeted high-throughput sequencing, and BRCA1 promoter 112 hypermethylation was evaluated using a targeted DNA methylation assay. Tumors 113 without BRCAloss (BRCA1/2 somatic or germline mutation, or BRCA1 promoter 114 hypermethylation) were tested for Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and 115 CCNE1-amplification using shallow whole genome sequencing and dedicated 116 algorithms (see Methods). We categorized 174 tumors (S. Fig. 1a) into four clinically 117 and molecularly relevant groups (BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous Recombination 118 Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification) 17.

The intricate interplay between immune co-279 infiltration, spatial organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a 280 coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the necessity of 281 integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed 282 spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME. 283 CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor 284 ecosystem features 285 To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and 286 prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom 287 forest), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (Fig. 3a). The 288 primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-289 dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA 290 robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical 291 features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation 292

10 in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent 293 overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and 294 balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a 295 prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a 296 sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical 297 features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the 298 previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus 299 estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate 300 multidimensional data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient 301 and molecular profiles (Fig. 3a). 302 We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the 4,644,485 single 303 cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (Fig. 1e, i; S. Fig. 304 1g), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (Fig. 1j), resulting in 43 feature 305 representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features 306 that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into three equally 307 sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (S. Fig. 3a,b).

Fig. 4a). Overall, these results demonstrate that HGSC tumors 386 with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more immune infiltration in the 387 TME. 388

We identified the top seven ligand–receptor interactions across 501 CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, Tregs, and DCs (Fig. 5h). Interestingly, IFN signaling and 502 MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells. 503 DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. 504 We also observed TGF-β1 interactions across the cell populations, which may indicate 505 the induction of immune tolerance 45. Similarly, in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics 506 dataset from the TSI (Fig. 5i), we observed a significant correlation between tumor-507 specific MHCII ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, 508 MPZL, IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (Fig. 5j). In addition, VEGFA, one of the 509 predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, 510 suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at 511 spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (Fig. 5j).

In conclusion, MHCII 512 expressing cancer cells elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune 513 cells in the surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with 514 activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways. 515

17 Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR 516 diversity and enhanced immunotherapy response in vitro 517 Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when antigen-518 loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous misfolded proteins 519 46. To explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with specific antigen 520 presentation, we focused on previously identified antigens detected via HLA 521 ligandome analysis in HGSC 47 (Fig. 6a). We found that the deconvoluted expression 522 of MHCII in cancer cells is indeed associated with the expression of most antigen-523 encoding genes in the TCGA dataset (Fig. 6b). However, in our scRNA-seq dataset, 524 only four specific antigen genes (UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN) were expressed 525 in the cancer cells, and associated with tumor MHCII expression (Fig. 6c). In our 526 GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also observed positive associations of 527 MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-528 encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at the TSI (Fig. 6d). 529 530 To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in HGSC, we 531 performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples 532 alongside TCR repertoire analysis based on bulk RNA-seq (Fig. 6e).

MHCII molecules in HGSC, we 531 performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples 532 alongside TCR repertoire analysis based on bulk RNA-seq (Fig. 6e). We identified the 533 presence of previously reported genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial 534 transcriptomics datasets that showed significant correlations and were consistently 535 observed across the patients (Fig. 6f). Moreover, the genes enriched in samples with 536 a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells were significantly enriched in cellular 537 components related to junctions, adhesion, the extracellular matrix, and cell–cell 538 contact zones, as well as in biological processes involved in protein localization and 539 regulation (Fig. 6g,h). To explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen 540 presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We observed a 541 significantly increased number of T cell receptor clones—specifically TRAD and TRB 542 —in samples with a high MHCII cancer proportion (Fig. 6i). 543 544 Given this association with enhanced T cell presence, we next investigated whether 545 tumor MHCII status influences CD8+ T-cell responses to immunotherapy using our 546 previously established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional patient-547 derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) 16. For this, we analysed flow cytometry 548

18 data from drug perturbation assays of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five chemo-549 treated tumor samples (Fig. 6j). The iPDCs were stratified into MHCII-high and MHCII-550 low groups based on baseline HLA-DR expression in EpCAM+ cancer cells compared 551 to immune and myeloid populations (see Methods and S. Fig. 6a,b). Notably, MHCII-552 high tumors exhibited significantly greater CD8+ T-cell cytotoxic responses to 553 pembrolizumab (Fig. 6k, S. Table 11). Sample-by-sample analysis revealed a 554 statistically significant cytotoxicity gain (p < 0.05) between baseline and 555 pembrolizumab-treated conditions in the MHCII-high group (Fig. 6l). 556 Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via 557 MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying 558 spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic 559 MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the 560 context of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade. 561 DISCUSSION 562 We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026 563 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical 564 samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying 565 phenotypic compositions.

the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical 564 samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying 565 phenotypic compositions. These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor 566 cell phenotypic profiles and clinicomolecular groups, underscoring the role of 567 molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the 568 two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer 569 cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI 570 displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 571 Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional 572 single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through 573 spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that 574 MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate 575 immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with 576 enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and 577 antigen-presenting cells.

hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with 576 enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and 577 antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize 578 MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune 579 surveillance 48,49, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T 580

19 cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 50. While IFN expression in 581 tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests 582 it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 51. Through a robust spatial 583 TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within 584 immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. 585 Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced 586 CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively 587 present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII 588 and IFN-γ expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic 589 MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and 590 antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in Fig. 7, 591 support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ 592 T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated 593 with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 594 MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary 595 pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 52.

in HGSC. 594 MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary 595 pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 52. In 596 HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs undergoing 597 whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis 53. 598 Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved 599 response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-600 negative breast cancer 41,50,54,55. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-601 L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated 602 only modest benefits 56. In contrast, our in vitro analysis of immunotherapy responses 603 in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might 604 enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of 605 CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our study revealed that other 606 immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in 607 tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical 608 exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients 609 with HGSC 16.

th high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical 608 exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients 609 with HGSC 16. Moreover, we showed that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in 610 MHCII-expressing tumors, and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR 611 clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While tumor-612 intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity cycle via active 613

20 antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches should be developed for patients 614 with tumors lacking MHCII expression. In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas 615 uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the 616 clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic 617 approaches and patient stratification. 618 Study limitations 619 We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for 620 future refinement and exploration. The retrospective design and reliance on archival 621 tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity, 622 limiting some of the statistical comparisons between patients. Our protein marker 623 panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell 624 types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there 625 is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have 626 matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning 627 models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further 628 improve predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable 629 hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger prospective patient 630 cohorts.

24 Whole slide cohort description 734 We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical 735 trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from patients during interval 736 debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection criteria for these samples included HR-737 proficient status, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-738 free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease 739 (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent 740 IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based 741 on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-742 PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis. 743 Molecular profile assignment 744 For the TMA cohort, we first identified BRCA1/2 mutation and BRCA1 promoter 745 methylation status of each sample as previously described 57. For patients without a BRCA1/2 germline mutation, both 748 BRCA1/2 somatic mutations and BRCA1 promoter hypermethylation were assessed 749 using DNA extracted from FFPE tumor samples.

The cancer meta-clusters were named according 857 to the expression of relevant markers in each one (Ext. material - Fig. 2). 858 Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis 859 For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality control, we 860 computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30µm, using the function 861 tl.spatial_count of the package scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) 69. We clustered the cells 862

28 based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods 863 (RCNs), by using k-means and a k=40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the 864 package scimap. Every cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value 865 of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick 70 866 (Ext. material - Fig. 3). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods 867 of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis using a radius of 868 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach 869 (Ext. material - Fig. 3). The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as 870 the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor. 871 Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis 872 To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity of the tumor, 873 we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 874 IDS HGSC samples with different responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For the QC, we excluded from the analysis the spots with less 878 than 500 genes detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% 879 of hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat's 880 SCTransform function. 881 882 Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an expert 883 pathologist as ‘Cancer’, ‘TSI’ (tumor-stroma interface with partial tumor and stromal 884 morphology), ‘Immune-Stromal’ and ‘other’. The models' training process employs 929 nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training 930 and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 931 30 data partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model 932 robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results 933 when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive 934 (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (S. Fig. 3n). For each nested cross-validation, using the 935 training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the 936 model (min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, n_estimators). The Bayesian 937 optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ 938 values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine 939 cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-940 folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-941 validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This 942 step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training 943 set.

ted recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This 942 step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training 943 set. Finally, we evaluate 945 the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop 946 (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median 947 performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are 948 reported. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics tumor profiling. 1462 Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC profiles: BRCAloss, 1463 homologous recombination deficient (HRD-BRCAwt), homologous recombination 1464 proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification (CCNE1amp), using methylation assay, 1465 targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor 1466 microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 1467

49 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and 1468 extract single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline 1469 (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming 1470 to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles. Our results were 1471 validated using statistical methods, followed by further biological analysis for 1472 interpretation, both incorporating external cohorts. b Antibody panel employed for t-1473 CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. c A bar plot displaying the composition of cancer 1474 cells, stromal cells, myeloid cells, lymphocytes, and unassigned cells per tumor. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a 1554 key prognostic feature in HGSC 1555 a A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined 1556 targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 1557 BRCAloss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to 1558 the target (for example, BRCAloss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation 1559 iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding 1560 cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to 1561 the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the 1562 training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold 1563 cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target 1564 classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either BRCAloss or HRP tumors) 1565 and its most predictive features are calculated.

on the test set to predict the target 1564 classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either BRCAloss or HRP tumors) 1565 and its most predictive features are calculated.
