### ***Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis***

To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity
of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial
transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different
responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data,
we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices
in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341). For the QC,
we excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes
detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of
hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using
Seurat\'s SCTransform function.

Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an
expert pathologist as 'Cancer', 'TSI' (tumor-stroma interface with
partial tumor and stromal morphology), 'Immune-Stromal' and 'other'.
Spots annotated as 'other' have been excluded from the analysis. Within
'Immune-Stromal' spots, we have distinguished an additional type -
'TSI2', defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to
either 'Cancer' or 'TSI' spots. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109). The MHCII signature

. Next, we assessed the activity of
MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell
score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109). The MHCII signature
comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed
MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the
median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to
chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor
response was adopted from Böhm et al, 2015
[(49)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?hhEDW9). An IFN-γ signature,
obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene
set collection (RRID:SCR_016863), was also quantified per spot.
Statistical significance has been computed using a two-sided Wilcoxon
Rank Sum test.

### ***CEFIIRA: Machine learning supervised classification***

CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the
identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter
tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training
and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent
overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a
proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3)

on multiple data partitions to prevent
overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a
proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3)
Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen
data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to
minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features
using feature elimination and feature permutations to elucidate how
accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly
shuffled, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested
cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation.

The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had
the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker
expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); 2)
three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled
using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in
a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when
the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS
the model included patients' clinical information -- FIGO stage and age
at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological
feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In

cal information -- FIGO stage and age
at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological
feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In
addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or
Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine
learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was
employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into
the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.

To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm
for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the
PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695,
[[https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret]{.underline}](https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret)).
We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of:
BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected
Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance
(weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (**S.Table
15**). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to determine the
number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations.

diction (weighted-average AUC
and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running
independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold
cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct
models for two classes (**S.Table 16**).

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python
scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) for binary classification of
cells into clinicomolecular groups
([https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA]{.underline}). The models\'
training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop
iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using
the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 30 data
partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model
robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve
similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based
on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (**S.Fig.3e**).
For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian
optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model
(*min_samples_split*, *min_samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of
hyperparameters' values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean

samples_leaf*, *n_estimators*). The Bayesian
optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of
hyperparameters' values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean
weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each
iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three
independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a
cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on
classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting
the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess
the importance of these selected features in predicting the target
variable through the computation of the mean decrease accuracy scores.
By randomly shuffling the values of each feature and observing the
resulting change in model performance, MDA scores quantify the
contribution of individual features to the model\'s accuracy. Finally,
we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set
partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell
level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard
deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported.
Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells,
Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified

### **GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis** 

We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors
obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy.
Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024
[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?qB1YRI), and is publicly
available at
[[(https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672)).
The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the
tumor--stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and
stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were
initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and
IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the
TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data

o-localization of CD8+ T cells and
IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the
TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data
had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed
normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by
variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using
the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206).

Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic
deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene
expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
([[https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism]{.underline}](https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism)),
a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial
transcriptomics datasets (RRID:SCR_027499). This method not only
estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles
for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw
scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577,
along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we
downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final
dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in
resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were
grouped into broader categories.

ding a final
dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in
resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were
grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T
cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B
cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90
endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late
erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient.
Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with
low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both
'cell types' and 'cell states'.

We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA)
scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB,
HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1,
HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific
deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess
the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we
correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from
BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y \~
exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating 'Patient' as a covariate (y
\~ exp(x) \| Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the
same tumor.

### ***Ligand-receptor analysis***

To predict ligand--receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data
based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing
them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq
analysis). MultiNicheNetR
([[https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git]{.underline}](https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git))
analysis was performed to assess ligand--receptor interactions between
immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T
cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions), comparing high
and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the
analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of
five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were
required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion
of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates
were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log
fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized

nalysis was used to identify ligand and
receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log
fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized
ligand--receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions
enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These
candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics
dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We
correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor
deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma
segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs
derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple
testing.

### ***Immunopeptidomics***

We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC
samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to
the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the
interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more
information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was
performed as previously described
[(50)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?7Lf2IK) with minor
modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed
on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by

ero.org/google-docs/?7Lf2IK) with minor
modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed
on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by
mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell
strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected
to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer
composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors
(Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich,
I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at
500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at
25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was
applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher),
pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of
anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the
manufacturer's coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen
capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.
The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the
following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM
NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were

150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM
NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM
Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were
dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges
(Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in
0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were
eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1%
TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The
eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using
the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
into a single sample.

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto
an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner
diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute
2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a
stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1
min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B

40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1
min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was
finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B
for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B
over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker),
with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs
cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired
with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the
minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at \< 700 m/z
or 3 Th \>800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000,
considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was
lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59
eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard
polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to
exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using
a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard
(ThermoFisher Scientific).

Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational
suite (RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome

(RRID:SCR_022864,
[[https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe]{.underline}](https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe)).
Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome
(UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on
2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes.
MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC
peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications:
enzyme specificity was set to 'non-specific' and 'null', allowing up to
two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and
30 amino acids, a mass range of 700--5000 Da, and charge states from +1
to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a
variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches
and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher
(https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher), applying a false discovery
rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and
peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant
(https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run
normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction
using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with
default settings.

ween-runs (MBR) option.

Peptides' searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction

### ***Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation***

Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the
samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq. We especially
focus on the somatic mutation module (PCGR,
[[https://github.com/sigven/pcgr]{.underline}](https://github.com/sigven/pcgr)),
which contains tumor purity and ploidy estimation, variant
classification (somatic SNVs/InDels and copy number aberrations), and
tumor mutational burden (TMB) estimation. TMB quantifies a tumor\'s
total somatic mutations per megabase (Mb), and was computed with command
"*-estimate_tmb*", with other settings of "*tmb_dp_min*", "*tmb_af_min*"
and "*tmb_ad_min*" as default. Among the output of iMTB reports, tumor
purity was used to verify the result of PRISM deconvolution. TMB was
used to show the genomic alterations related to immune proportions.

### ***Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data***

RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for
immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with the
Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than
100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data
were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the iCAN
study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM
(RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale
factors, and cell-type--specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor,
stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for
PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell
RNA-seq dataset from [(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?s0IsHW),
which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29
patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the
tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After
decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate
an immune--stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and

s for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After
decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate
an immune--stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and
each compartment (tumor and immune--stromal) were computed using the
PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such
as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition
weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment
within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the
expression level expected for each cell type independent of its
abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted
for the 35 genes identified from immunopeptidomics. Patients were
stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched
t-CycIF imaging (as described in the Immunopeptidomics section). For
each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled
expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity
of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by
comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the
median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune--stromal
compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values
closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.

across both the tumor and immune--stromal
compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values
closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled
expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean
scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low
samples.

### ***TCR repertoire analysis***

We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC
samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples
(total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study (as described in previous
section). TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7,
14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted
using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725), following the RNA-seq workflow
([[https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/]{.underline}](https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/))
with default settings. We used 'mixcr analyze rnaseq', followed by
'mixcr exportQc align' and 'mixcr exportQc chainUsage' for quality
control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq
(non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully
aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones,
with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in

low percentage of reads successfully
aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones,
with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in
7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group).
Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly
represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal
diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R
package (RRID:SCR_023089). We employed the 'repExplore' function with
the \"volume\" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups
stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification
done in Immunopeptidomics): 1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone
counts for TRAD and TRB); 2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts
for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers
between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon
rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the
Holm method.

To reveal how the blockade of MHCII affects the response to ICB, frozen
tumor-derived samples from three patients with high HLA-DPB1 expression
were selected from the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective HGSC cohort based on RNA
expression in deconvoluted with PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) tumor fraction.
The tumor tissue had been enzymatically dissociated prior to
cryopreservation. For iPDC generation, thawed cells were embedded as
droplets in an omentum-derived extracellular matrix
[(32)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?bpgwyb). Cultures were
treated with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination
treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) (RRID:AB_395939) or isotype
control was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently
added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were
harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions.
Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3,
CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ)
markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and
doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating. Data were

voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in
FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a
consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in
FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and
subsequently analyzed in R. Statistical analyses were performed in
RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build 418, RRID:SCR_000432) using R (version
4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from FlowJo were processed
for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFN-γ, Granzyme B)
along with associated metadata, such as patient id and condition.
Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical comparisons
between conditions. For each patient, the median fluorescent intensity
for the IFN-γ, and Granzyme B was calculated across all cells within
each cell type and experimental condition. The resulting median
intensity values were then used to calculate the log2​ fold change for
each patient, cell type, and marker. Three distinct log2​ fold change
comparisons were performed: pembrolizumab vs. control, anti-HLA +
pembrolizumab vs.

the log2​ fold change for
each patient, cell type, and marker. Three distinct log2​ fold change
comparisons were performed: pembrolizumab vs. control, anti-HLA +
pembrolizumab vs. pembrolizumab, and anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs.
control. Finally, the mean of these individual patient log2​ fold change
values was calculated for each comparison across all patients to
determine the pooled treatment effect.

### ***[Cytokine profiling via LEGENDplex]{.mark}™ [assay]{.mark}***

Supernatants from the cultures used to assess the effect of MHC-II
blockade in the responses to pembrolizumab treatment were collected at
the experimental endpoint (day 5), clarified by centrifugation (1500
rpm, 10 min, 4 °C), and stored at --80 °C until the time of assay.
Secreted cytokines were quantified using the LEGENDplex™ Human CD8/NK
Panel (13-plex) (BioLegend, catalog #741187, lot #B409342). The assay
was carried out according to the manufacturer's recommended protocol.
Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa^TM^ cell analyzer (BD
Biosciences), and the resulting data were processed using the
LEGENDplex™ Data Analysis Software. Final plots were generated using
custom R scripts.

### ***Survival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival
(RRID:SCR_021137).

vival analysis***

To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression
models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival
(RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by
calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The
proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total
cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more
than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, **S.Fig.1b**) were selected for
survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell
phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune
populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core
post-preprocessing (n=244, **S.Fig.1b**). Patient stratification based
on cell population used the cohort\'s median, defining high (above
median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split
into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66,
and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same
approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular
profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only
high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.

### ***Statistical analyses***

We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3
(RRID:SCR_001905).

set survival analysis, only
high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.

### ***Statistical analyses***

We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3
(RRID:SCR_001905). The difference in abundance between the two groups
was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated
that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different
groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The
correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations.
For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and
Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were
considered statistically significant.

## **FIGURE LEGENDS**

### **Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.**

**a** Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics
tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC
profiles: *BRCA*loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-*BRCA*wt),
homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification
(*CCNE1amp*), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and
shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was
assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps).
High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract
single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline
(CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical
covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and
molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical
methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation,
both incorporating external cohorts. **b** Antibody panel employed for
t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers.

### **Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC** 

**a** A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline 'CEFIIRA'.
Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example,
predicting if cancer cells belong to either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors.
Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target
(for example, *BRCA*loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation
iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their
corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the
remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation.

set. Subsequently, for each
iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with
several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to
predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to
either *BRCA*loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are
calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested
cross-validations, the model's median accuracy and consensus critical
features are identified. **b-c** Heatmap displaying the median accuracy

We sincerely thank the reviewers for their encouraging and invaluable
comments. We have now addressed all the comments and believe that the
manuscript has been significantly strengthened. Below are the
point-by-point responses (in blue).

The main improvements in the revised manuscript:

1. Improved resolution of tumor-specific expression of MHCII - with
 > **Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data, additional spatial
 > proteomics representations, scRNAseq and imaging data analysis**
 > excluding the possibility of artefacts/spill over and
 > demonstrating true MHCII expression in the tumor cells.

2. Additional compelling evidence supporting the role of tumor MHCII in
 > HGSC taking into account **tumor genomic features, anatomical site
 > variation and associated immune cell signatures.**

3. Additional validation on the effect of tumor MHCII independently of
 > MHCI.

4. New analysis demonstrating the **technical robustness of CEFIIRA**,
 > excluding the effect of overall feature variability explaining the
 > model performance.

5. Improved malignant-compartment specific immunopeptidomic analysis
 > showing **MHCII antigens linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion**
 > providing the mechanistic background of MHCII expression on tumor
 > cells.

6. **New data from functional ex vivo experiments confirming the
 > mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation**. using a novel

kground of MHCII expression on tumor
 > cells.

6. **New data from functional ex vivo experiments confirming the
 > mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation**. using a novel
 > platform of patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we
 > demonstrated that MHCII-high tumors exhibit enhanced CD8+ T-cell
 > cytotoxicity in response to PD-1 inhibition, and that MHCII
 > blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production.
 > These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency of T-cell
 > activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for optimizing
 > immunotherapy strategies and improving patient outcomes in cancer
 > treatment.

This work presents several key novelties and impactful findings. Unlike
prior studies, which have only associated immune hotspot activation with
prognosis, we establish a direct link between intra-tumoral immune
hotspots and MHCII expression in cancer cells with single-cell spatial
resolution. We show, for the first time, that tumor MHCII expression is
spatially connected to localized immune activation and that immune cells
adjacent to MHCII+ cancer cells are tumor-reactive and more activated.
Additionally, our research reveals the spatially confined effects of
tumor-stroma interface composition, independent of tumor molecular
features. Crucially, we provide mechanistic evidence that tumors with

reveals the spatially confined effects of
tumor-stroma interface composition, independent of tumor molecular
features. Crucially, we provide mechanistic evidence that tumors with
high MHCII expression respond better to immune checkpoint blockade, with
this effect abrogated by MHCII blockade. These findings open new
therapeutic avenues, such as engineered immunopeptides or vaccines, and
highlight the importance of MHCII as a biomarker for more effective
patient stratification in HGSC. This work offers strong support for
future clinical validation in both retrospective and prospective trials.

**[Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):]{.underline}**

Anniina Farkkila and colleagues report a single-cell spatial analysis of
metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer from 280 patients, including
929 single-cell maps. The authors identified heterogeneous
immune-environments at the tumor-stroma interface that are predictive of
clinical outcomes. Among these predictive factors, the authors newly
identified tumor cell MHCII expression as a major predictor of superior
outcomes and validated their findings with independent datasets.

In addition to a technological tour of force and the use of an extensive
cohort of patients, the main strength of the study lies in the
identification of tumor-intrinsic IFNG-dependent MHCII expression as

gical tour of force and the use of an extensive
cohort of patients, the main strength of the study lies in the
identification of tumor-intrinsic IFNG-dependent MHCII expression as
marker of T cell-mediated immune control of malignant progression in
human ovarian cancer. This is novel and biologically interesting. In
addition, the finding that lymphocyte (T and B cells) infiltration loses
predictive value after chemotherapy opens new avenues to investigate the
immunobiology of human ovarian cancer, the basis of which have been
established for treatment-naïve tumors before clinical practices
changed. Increased frequencies of immunologically cold tumors after
chemotherapy suggest a deleterious effect of chemotherapy on existing
anti-tumor immune responses.

In this manuscript, the authors employ multiplexed imaging along with
other datasets to profile a large number of HGSC samples. They identify
various immune populations and define cellular neighborhoods. Several
lymphocyte populations are found to be linked to better OS and PFS.
Using their newly developed CEFIIRA tool, they discover HLA-II
expression on tumor cells as a key feature associated with longer

The manuscript is strong in its scale and the use of a wide variety of
datasets, 253 patients are profiled in depth. The presented findings are
consistent with each other and align with previous observations from
other tumor entities. Overall, the presented analyses are of high
quality and presented in an appropriate manner. Most of the claims are
well supported by the presented data, with suggestions for further

communities.

Specific comments

Tumor cell expression of MHCII emerges as a top feature predicting OS,
however it remains elusive how much of this prognostic value is
independent of other associated variables. The authors should report an
adjusted model that adjusts for age, stage, therapy and molecular
profile. Another way of showing this would be to run their CEFIIRA
analysis leaving out feature classes (e.g. MHCII expression) and
reporting loss of predictive performance.

We thank the reviewer for this comment. The adjusted Cox model for OS,
adjusting for age, stage, therapy and molecular profiles is shown in

orting loss of predictive performance.

We thank the reviewer for this comment. The adjusted Cox model for OS,
adjusting for age, stage, therapy and molecular profiles is shown in
**Fig 3k,** showing that tumor MHCII remains predictive with HR 0.76 (CI
0.62-0.93). To further clarify our approach, we confirm that CEFIIRA
uses an established, cross-validated recursive feature elimination
(RFECV) to **identify the most informative predictors while adjusting
for the clinicomolecular factors**. After feature selection, we compute
permutation-based mean decrease in accuracy (MDA) scores, which quantify
the contribution of each variable by measuring the drop in model
performance when that variable is randomly permuted. This approach
leverages out-of-bag samples to provide an unbiased estimate of variable
importance. While CEFIIRA is not designed to produce traditional
regression-based adjusted effect estimates (e.g., hazard ratios), the
combination of RFECV and permutation importance allows us to assess the
relative contribution of each feature **while accounting for
correlations among variables.** We have now further described our
feature selection and interpretation steps in the manuscript\'s Methods
section (lines **934-935, 982-985**).

reported to express HLAII previously and the authors present a lot of

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. We are very
committed to ensuring that the cancer cell--specific signals we report
are not confounded by spillover from immune cells. To address this, we
have performed several new **analyses in our scRNAseq and spatial
proteomics data, and also produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics
data.**

First, we quantified the degree of potential spillover in cancer cells
and assessed its correlation with MHC-II signal, as suggested. We did
not detect any correlation between CD3 or CD11b expression and MHC-II
levels in cancer cells, and we now include these results in a new
Supplementary Fig. 3g. Because high MHC-II expression could
theoretically arise from a combined spillover of CD3+ and CD11b+ immune
cells, we also tested the correlation between MHCII and the maximum
signal of these two markers. Again, no association was observed (see top
panel below).

In a complementary analysis, we examined the correlation between CD11b
signal in cancer cells and CD11b signal in neighboring immune cells

ion was observed (see top
panel below).

In a complementary analysis, we examined the correlation between CD11b
signal in cancer cells and CD11b signal in neighboring immune cells
(within 20 μm) and found a strong correlation (bottom left panel),
confirming the sensitivity of our approach to detect spillover when it
occurs. In contrast, this level of correlation was not observed between
MHC-II in neighboring immune cells and cancer cells (bottom right
panel), indicating that cancer cell MHC-II expression is not solely
explained by spillover. Moreover, in sc-RNAseq, we demonstrated that
there are no expressions of immune related genes in cancer cells
expressing MHCII (new Supplementary Fig. 4c).

Finally, we have now produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data
from a high-MHCII tumor followed by post-xenium tCycIF staining, and
show that CD74, a transcript of the MHCII complex is robustly expressed
in the tumor cells (**new Figure 3f**).

Although robust pixel-level spillover removal methods are not yet
available and are impacted due to the close proximity of immune and
cancer cells, **our multi-modal analyses collectively support that a
substantial component of the MHC-II signal detected in cancer cells
reflects a true cancer-intrinsic expression.** We have now also added
these new results to the manuscript lines **321-323**).

Prediction models performed better in HRP-like tumors than in HRD-like
counterparts. Is this explained by technical or biological factors?

We thank the reviewer for pointing out this observation. We evaluated
both technical and biological explanations for the observed better
performance of prediction models in HRP-like tumors. CEFIIRA was built
to minimise technical bias through i) strict sample-wise data
partitioning (preventing leakage), ii) stratified splits balanced by
therapy and molecular subtype, iii) nested cross-validation with
parameter tuning, iv) independent validation on unseen data, and v)
repeated (30×) consensus estimation. These controls make it unlikely
that technical artifacts explain the performance difference.

We also examined whether intrinsic biological heterogeneity might
contribute to the performance gap in OS predictions. To assess this, we

acts explain the performance difference.

We also examined whether intrinsic biological heterogeneity might
contribute to the performance gap in OS predictions. To assess this, we
quantified the variability of CEFIIRA input features within HRP-like and
HRD-like tumors by computing Shannon entropy and the Jensen--Shannon
(JS) divergence between the two groups (see graph of Shannon entropy and
Table with below). Using permutation testing (50 permutations), we did
not detect high differences in entropy or JS divergence for these
features, suggesting that **overall feature variability does not explain
the performance discrepancy supporting the robustness of our approach.**

These results strongly suggest that technical factors are unlikely to
account for the observed differences in predictive performance. The most
compelling explanation points to intrinsic biological differences
between HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Remarkably, MHCII stood out as the
top feature, exhibiting the highest entropy difference between the HRD
and HRP groups, with significantly elevated values in HRD-like tumors.
**This underscores the substantial biological heterogeneity within
HRD-like tumors, further reinforcing the power of our integrative
approach in uncovering critical, clinically relevant features from our
multi-omics dataset.**

We thank the reviewer for this question. CEFIIRA can technically
accommodate correlated features and does so through RFECV-based feature
selection and permutation importance, both of which evaluate feature
contributions while accounting for multicollinearity. However, we chose
to build separate models for tumor and immune cells primarily for
interpretability. When multiple cell types are combined into a single
feature table, CEFIIRA identifies the most predictive features across
the entire dataset without distinguishing whether a given feature
originates from tumor or immune compartments. This makes it difficult to

fies the most predictive features across
the entire dataset without distinguishing whether a given feature
originates from tumor or immune compartments. This makes it difficult to
attribute the source of the predictive signal and to draw biologically
meaningful conclusions about cell-type--specific contributions. We have
now added this justification into the manuscript in lines **300-302**.

Lastly, the authors included a functional experiment involving iPDC
co-cultures to determine the effects of HLA-II expression on tumor cells
on immune cells. However, this functional link could be further
strengthened by blocking HLA-II to show that this has functional
consequences for immune cell behavior.

We thank the reviewer for this excellent suggestion. Using our ex-vivo
model [(14)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?lCHYa3), we now
utilized a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII to investigate the
dependency of T cell activation on MHCII signalling. Prior to the
experiment, we confirmed that the iPDCs contained minimal contributions
of myeloid cells on the day of readout (day 5, Figure below shown for
three iPDCs).

Notably, in our new data, MHCII blockade completely abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**new Fig. 7d**) and Granzyme B
(**new Fig. 7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,

, MHCII blockade completely abolished
PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (**new Fig. 7d**) and Granzyme B
(**new Fig. 7e**) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally,
multiplexed cytokine profiling of supernatant from the iPDCs confirmed
the loss of extracellular cytokine production after MHCII blockade
(**new Fig. 7f**). **These functional assays demonstrate the critical
role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and local immune responses,
supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII.**
This has been added also to manuscript lines **563-571**).

[Dear Dr. Michele Hartsough,]{.mark}

[We are pleased to submit our revised manuscript titled
**"**]{.mark}*Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian
cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor
ecosystems and clinical outcomes*[**"**.]{.mark}

We sincerely thank you and the reviewers for their positive comments. We
have now addressed all the remaining comments.

[Thank you for working with us on our manuscript. We look forward to the
opportunity to contribute to *Cancer Discovery*.]{.mark}

[Yours sincerely,]{.mark}

vector_chunks.text:
## Point-by-point rebuttal letter

We thank the editor and reviewer for the final comments. We have now
addressed all these and finished the manuscript edits accordingly.

*Editorial comments*

We have updated the article file, Figures and Supplementary material
based on editors' comments and the requirements of the journal.

*Reviewer comment*

Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author):

The authors have addressed most of my comments to satisfaction, I only
have one remark.

The authors present new data supporting the notion that MHCII is
expressed by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging.
While this is appreciated and supported by their functional experiments,
I am wondering why in Supp 3g and in the point-by-point response, the
values for MHCII on cancer cells don't line up between the different
correlation analyses (i.e. the correlations with CD11b, MHCII and CD3).
In the MHCII in cancer vs. neighbor plot there are lots of cancer MHCII
values between 11 and 15 while these are missing from the cancer MHCII
vs max(CD3d or CD11b). Where are the different input cells used here?
The authors should show this data for all tumor cells.

We thank the reviewer for this comment and agree that key aspects of the
analysis were not sufficiently described previously, which may have
caused confusion. This confusion was due to the downsampling of the data
prior to analysis.

t key aspects of the
analysis were not sufficiently described previously, which may have
caused confusion. This confusion was due to the downsampling of the data
prior to analysis. We have now performed a new in-silico experiment
supporting the validity of our downsampling approach, and added a
clarification of the analysis details to the figure legend, as detailed
in our response Importantly, we have now added a new **Supplementary
Figure 3g (new S5g)** with further details of the downsampling, and
added the statistics calculated using all cancer cells.

Our previous analysis was performed using a random downsampling of 500
cancer cells. We adopted this approach because when all cancer cells
were included (N ≈ 2 million), the high dispersion in the data precluded
reliable visualization of correlated trends, as shown in the two figures
below that include all the cancer cells. Importantly, according to the
central limit theorem for correlated data, random sampling preserves the
statistical properties of the original distribution. This principle has
also been discussed and demonstrated in the context of spatial tumor
profiling by Jia-Ren Lin et al. (2023; PMID: 36669472), specifically in
Figures 2F and 2H.

To assess the robustness of this downsampling strategy, we performed a
new in-silico experiment. Downsampling was conducted 10,000 times, with

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/Re-Submission after provisional acceptance 2026/Point-by-point rebuttal letter- CD2026.md
vector_chunks.text:
specifically in
Figures 2F and 2H.

To assess the robustness of this downsampling strategy, we performed a
new in-silico experiment. Downsampling was conducted 10,000 times, with
500 cancer cells sampled per replicate. For each replicate, the Pearson
correlation coefficient (R) was calculated between the expression of the
indicated markers and MHCII in the sampled cancer cells. The mean R
across the 10,000 replicates is reported together with the corresponding
95% confidence intervals (CI) in the next table. In addition, the mean
p-value and its confidence intervals for testing the deviation of R from
zero. As observed, the mean correlations and confidence intervals across
replicates fall within the range of correlations previously reported in
Supplementary Figure 3g and are consistent with results obtained using
the full dataset. We therefore consider that displaying a downsampling
is a more faithful representation of the data for visualization
purposes. We have now added a new **Supplementary Figure 3g (new S5g)**
with further details of the downsampling, and added the statistics
calculated using all cancer cells.

## **Abstract**

The tumor microenvironment in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC)
is a complex network of malignant--host cell interactions, yet its
orchestration remains poorly understood. We present a single-cell
spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating
high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Analyzing 929
single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with diverse cell
compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the
tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find
that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged
survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct
spatial transcriptomic datasets, along with immunopeptidomic analysis,
confirms that MHCII expression correlates with immune activation,

econvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct
spatial transcriptomic datasets, along with immunopeptidomic analysis,
confirms that MHCII expression correlates with immune activation,
antigen presentation, and TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived
immuno-oncology platform, we demonstrate that tumor MHCII expression
associates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade,
while blocking MHCII inhibits this activation. Our atlas offers new
insights into immune activation, potentially improving patient
stratification in HGSC.

## **Statement of significance**

This study delivers the first large-scale single-cell spatial atlas of
HGSC, revealing how tumor--immune organization shapes outcomes. We
identify tumor-intrinsic MHCII as a key driver of local immune
activation and immunotherapy responsiveness, providing a mechanistic
biomarker that can immediately inform improved patient stratification
and therapeutic decision-making in ovarian cancer.

**INTRODUCTION**

The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising
cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components
and their interactions[(1)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?nfS5Pn).
The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding
microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes,
cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance.

ontinuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding
microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes,
cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade
serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of
ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic
instability[(2)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?UliKHh) and
significant tumor
heterogeneity[(3)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?1lvJx8). The
genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For
instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair
deficiency, such as those with *BRCA1/2* mutations, have been suggested
to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte
infiltration[(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jcEirr) and better
responses to combinations with
immunotherapies[(5,6)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?9vmQCT).
Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant
chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the
intricate interactions within the immune
compartment[(7--9)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?Yzi8KV),
resulting in spatially confined T-cell
exhaustion[(10)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?EjZUnX). Therefore,
it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and
molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve

rg/google-docs/?EjZUnX). Therefore,
it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and
molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve
patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for
patients with HGSC.

We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1
million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with
comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging
DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a
tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the
single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that
associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning
tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to
uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of
cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at
the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of
immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate
immune hotspots marked by increased activation, and tumor reactivity
impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles.
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition
ex-vivo.

impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles.
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition
ex-vivo. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune
cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the
way for targeted interventions in HGSC.

## **RESULTS**

### **Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors**

To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography,
clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic
profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing,
gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex
imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (**Fig.1a**). The
tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from
treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after
platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS
n=158). Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes,
were collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67
months) (**Table S1**). Most patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and
paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free
survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). We conducted a hierarchical

ients (87.5%) received carboplatin and
paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free
survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). We conducted a hierarchical
multidimensional analysis to categorize the 174 tumors (**Fig.S1a**)
into four clinically and molecularly relevant groups (*BRCAloss, HRD,
Homologous Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1
amplification*)[(11)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?dchEuz). The
rest (n=91) of the tumors were left "unassigned" as they could not be
reliably categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see
Methods).

To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used
hyperplexed imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF)
targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing tissue
microarrays (TMAs, **Fig.1b, Table S2**). Image processing and quality
filtering produced 929 single-cell maps of HGSC from 253 patients
(**Fig.1a, Fig.S1b**). In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485
high-quality single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for each
tumor. The cell phenotyping was based on the multidimensional protein
expression (**Fig.S1c**). We comprehensively validated the cell
phenotypes **(Fig.1c,d)** with visual assessment (**Fig.1e,f**). The
annotated cell phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles
corresponding to their respective cell lineages (**Fig.S1d**).

annotated cell phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles
corresponding to their respective cell lineages (**Fig.S1d**). The deep
cell phenotype annotation comprised two non-immune stromal populations
(annotated stromal) constituting 40% of the cells (CD31+ endothelial
cells and Vimentin+, α-SMA+ fibroblasts) and 15 immune populations
corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells, memory and
regulatory T cells, and Myeloid lineages such as macrophages,

abundance of CD8+ T and CD20+ B cells aligned with the expert
pathologists' annotations based on immunohistochemistry (IHC)
(**Fig.S1e,f**). The cells formed distinct visual clusters that aligned

(**Fig.1i, Fig.S1g**) and PDL1 expression (**Fig.S1g**), some of which
have been associated with long-term survival in

quantified the cell-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods (**Fig.1j**).

### **Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles** 

We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the
defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all
molecular groups did not reveal significant differences after multiple
hypothesis correction (**Fig.S4a, Table S3**). However, consistent with
previous reports [(4)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kxTuxY), we
observed that *BRCA*loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and
CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (**Fig.2a**).

//www.zotero.org/google-docs/?kxTuxY), we
observed that *BRCA*loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and
CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (**Fig.2a**).
Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed
exclusively in *BRCA1*loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples
(**Fig.2a**). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found
that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and
macrophages were enriched in IDS samples (**Fig.S4b**). Interestingly,
all lymphocyte populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated
significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (**Fig.2b;
Tables S4,5**). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages,
did not exhibit this co-infiltration (**Fig.2b**). These results imply
that lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated
process potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms.

We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with
clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the
abundance of lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival
(OS) (**Fig.2c**, **Fig.S4c**) and progression-free survival (PFS)
(**Fig.S4d**). The abundance of CD11c.MY and general myeloid cells also
showed association with longer PFS (**Fig.S4d**). Notably, in IDS

suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors related to chemotherapy exposure
beyond immune cell abundance are relevant for prognosis, as previously

the highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the

For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant

next performed multivariable Cox models using stricter inclusion

Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship between immune

**Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
chemotherapy exposure**

To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells
based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We
uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter
referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN; **Fig.2e-g**).
Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal (RCN01
to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells adjacent to
cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09 and RCN10)
and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial domains
included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial
cell-rich domain (RCN03; **Fig.2e-g**). The cancer spatial domains
included the phenotypically most heterogeneous neighbourhood (RCN04),
and four neighbourhoods were predominated by distinct tumor cell

included the phenotypically most heterogeneous neighbourhood (RCN04),

(RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11; **Fig.2e-g**). These spatial domains were

Spatial composition analysis revealed that several of the domains were

In contrast, the immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation

ancer cells displaying immunogenic

We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the
clinicomolecular profiles. The overall comparisons did not reveal
significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple

heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in

spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of

Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less abundant
in the IDS samples (**Fig.2k**) suggesting phenotypic convergence of
cancer cell populations following chemotherapy, consistent with our
previous observation[(7)](https://www.zotero.org/google-docs/?jfFZaD).
These results highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies
associated with the molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure.

Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical
outcomes. Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy
sequence (PDS/IDS), we identified three RCNs (RCN03, RCN09, RCN10)
significantly associated with both PFS and OS (**Fig.S4i,j**). Notably,
the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition emerged as a
key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across the entire
patient cohort (**Fig.2m**). Specifically, a high abundance of the
immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved prognosis,
whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated with poorer
outcomes (**Fig.2m**).

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial
domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes
within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse
spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The
intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial

### **CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features**

To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune
infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature
Importance Identification by RAndom forest), a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) framework (**Fig.3a**). The primary objective of
CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within
high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample
groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and
the identification of critical features (see Methods). Additionally, as
a versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional
data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and
molecular profiles (**Fig.3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the

ensional
data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and
molecular profiles (**Fig.3a**).

We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the
4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker
expressions (**Fig.1e,i; Fig.S1g**), morphological features and spatial
neighborhoods (**Fig.1j**), resulting in 43 feature representations per
cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features that
differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into
three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (**Fig.S5a,b**).
We generated CEFIIRA models for either cancer cells or the
immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (*BRCA*loss, HRD) or
HRP-like (HRP, *CCNE1*amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also
including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. This strategy
made it easier to interpret the predictive signal and dissect the
cell-type--specific contributions. As expected, the patient\'s age and
tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the
models (**Fig.S5c-e**). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited
better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors
in both PDS (**Fig.3b, Table S8**) and IDS (**Fig.3c, Table S8**), which
was not explained by overall feature variability (**Table S9**). These

an those trained on HRD-like tumors
in both PDS (**Fig.3b, Table S8**) and IDS (**Fig.3c, Table S8**), which
was not explained by overall feature variability (**Table S9**). These
results indicate that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with
differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal
single-cell spatial data. To account for anatomical site heterogeneity,
we trained CEFIIRA models exclusively on primary tumor sites
(adnex/ovary) and confirmed that the top predicted features remained
consistent (**Fig.S5f**).

### **Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival** 

Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as
one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples
(**Fig.S5c-e**). Moreover, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed
the highest fold-change enrichment from long-OS patients compared to
short-OS patients in HRP tumors (**Fig.3d,e; Table S8**). By contrast,
stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint
receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In
addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS
samples (**Fig.3d**). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in
cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring
cells at the TSI.

ker of long-OS in PDS
samples (**Fig.3d**). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in
cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring
cells at the TSI. However, the MHCII levels in cancer cells did not
correlate with CD3 or CD11b levels, confirming that this signal is not
due to technical spillover from neighboring T cells or myeloid cells
(**Fig.S5g**). Indeed, visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed
MHCII expression by cancer cells (**Fig.3f**). We also performed Xenium
single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which confirmed the co-localization
of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII complex, specifically on the
tumor cells (**Fig.3f**). In addition, MHCII was also the top predictive
marker in the immune-stromal cells in IDS samples (**Fig.3e)**.
Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII and immune-stromal
markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial proximity, are relevant
for OS prediction regardless of therapy sequence.

Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular
profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of
individual cells. We observed that PDS samples demonstrated superior
classification performance than IDS samples (**Fig.3g**; **Fig.S5h).**
In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher expression in
the HRD-like tumors (**Fig.3h; Fig.S5i**).

In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher expression in
the HRD-like tumors (**Fig.3h; Fig.S5i**). The CEFIIRA model to classify
*BRCA*loss or HRP cells achieved the highest predictive performance
(Median F1=0.77, **Fig.3g**), with MHCII being the most differential
feature displaying higher expression in *BRCA*loss cancer cells
