R1: List of Supplementary tables
R2: Index | Legend
R3: Table S1 | Description of clinical information in the multidimensional cohort. For seven tumors, there was not matched clinical information.
R5: Table S2 | List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the multidimensional cohort (N=265). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
R7: Table S3 | The median of the median proportions of each immune population per tumor across molecular profiles. First, the median proportion of each immune population was calculated per tumor (across cores); then, for each molecular profile, the median of these tumor-level medians was computed. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis. N of each group is reported in Supplementary Fig. S4a.
R9: Table S4 | Pearson correlation values of the median immune infiltration population across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per population a median immune infiltration was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)

relation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)
R11: Table S5 | False discovery rate (FDR) of the correlation test of the median immune infiltration correlation (Table S4)
R13: Table S6 | Pearson correlation values of the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251).
R15: Table S7 | False discovery rate (FDR) from the correlation test of the median RCN proportion across tumors (Table S6)
R17: Table S8 | Top predictive markers (from Supplementary Fig. 5c&d) for distinguishing long vs short overall survival (OS) HRDs and HRPs Cancer or Immune-Stromal cells, with reflected across corresponding predicted class median value of min-max scaled expression values after CEFIIRA preprocessing to highlight differences within the cell population. In addition, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.

ion, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.
R19: Table S9 | Quantified variability of CEFIIRA input features for OS models within HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Entropy indices in HRD and HRP tumors were calculated using Shannon entropy and Jensen–Shannon (JS) divergence. Each row represents the single-cell features used in the CEFIIRA models. The entropy difference between HRD-like and HRP-like tumors was assessed using a permutation test (50 folds), and the resulting p-value is reported.
R21: Table S10 | List of overexpressed genes in OVA-TCGA when comparing tumors with high cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.
R23: Table S11 | Results of differential expression analysis comparing tumors with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells in t-CycIF (N = 66) to those with a low proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells (N = 67) using the multidimensional cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented.

of MHCII+ cancer cells (N = 67) using the multidimensional cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented. Significance was assessed using a t-test, and p-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the false discovery rate (FDR). Genes were considered differentially expressed based on the following criteria: log2 fold change ≥ 1 or ≤ –1 and FDR < 10⁻³.
R25: Table S12 | Summary table of analysis performed on deconvoluted bulk RNAseq for 35 genes enriched in immunopeptidomic samples with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells and filtered based on healthy tissue HLA-II ligandome across three patient groups: with low (four samples), mid (four samples), and high (four samples) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Includes:
 - Median values of scaled expression for tumor and immune-stromal fractions in high group.
 - Median z-scores for tumor and immune-stromal fractions in high group.
 - Specificity of gene expression to the tumor fraction in the high group, assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor fraction with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal fractions combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
 - Fold change of tumor fraction expression in high vs.

combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.
 - Fold change of tumor fraction expression in high vs. the mean tumor fraction expression in low and mid groups.
R27: Table S13 | Granzyme B response of CD8⁺ T cells in iPDCs following treatment with pembrolizumab (PE, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (TI, 10 µg/mL), or their combination (PE+TI). Responses per sample were calculated as the mean difference between treated (days 2–5) and control (d0) samples and analyzed separately for MHCII-high and MHCII-low cancer sample groups. The mean log2FC across samples in each group is reported in the corresponding column. Statistical significance was assessed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Averaged changes and pembrolizumab-induced (PE) changes in Granzyme B response are also shown.
R29: Table S14 | List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the whole slide validation cohort (N=15). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
R31: Table S15 | Logic table to define cell phenotypes using TRIBUS. A value of one (1) is considered a marker expected in the cell phenotype.

f function to identify in experiment.
R31: Table S15 | Logic table to define cell phenotypes using TRIBUS. A value of one (1) is considered a marker expected in the cell phenotype. A value of zero (0) is considered as a not relevant value to define the given cell phenotype. A value of minus one (-1) corresponds to expected negative markers in the corresponding cell phenotype.
R33: Table S16 | Performance for different types of models for the classification of individual cancer cells in to the molecular profiles using the entire multidimensional dataset after quality filter (N=253).
R35: Table S17 | Performance of random forest CEFIIRA models for the prediction of molecular profiles of individual cancer cells from PDS tumors in the multidimensional cohort.

Objectives of the study & their impact

The project aims to discover how the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and MHC dysregulation facilitate immune escape in ovarian cancer:
Objective 1. Identify MHC patterns in HGSC subtypes and determine their clinical significance. 
Using t-CycIF, we profiled single-cell spatial landscapes of HGSC in 265 samples and characterized cellular communities and their interactions. Through machine-learning–guided feature extraction, we identified distinct MHC dysregulation patterns associated with specific HGSC molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.
Impact This work demonstrates that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is a key determinant of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness. The findings, validated across four independent cohorts, were submitted to Cancer Discovery (second author contribution) and provide strong evidence that spatially resolved antigen presentation programs influence prognosis and therapeutic response.

Objective 2. Elucidate molecular mechanisms of TME-dependent MHC dysregulation and tumor progression. 
To investigate how chemotherapy and TME remodeling influence MHC regulation, we integrated spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (NanoString GeoMx) in 41 tumor tissues (including rare paired pre- and post-chemotherapy samples).

egulation, we integrated spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (NanoString GeoMx) in 41 tumor tissues (including rare paired pre- and post-chemotherapy samples). Across 1,180 transcriptomic segments, we are identifying transcriptional programs associated with spatial immunophenotypes and resistance mechanisms.
Impact: This integrative approach enables the identification of molecular pathways underlying resistant spatial niches. By linking spatial immune architecture with gene-expression programs, we aim to reveal targetable vulnerabilities in chemotherapy-resistant HGSC.

Collectively, these objectives provide mechanistic insight into immune evasion in ovarian cancer and establish spatial MHC dysregulation as a biomarker-driven framework for personalized therapy and drug development.

Objective 2: Spatial MHC Dysregulation & Resistance Mechanisms
Data integration is performed across 41 tumors and 1,180 segments using tCycIF and GeoMx, with explicit ROI identification.
SPACEStat analyzes cell networks, including direct niche diagrams, molecular programs via heatmap, and validation.
Direct niche diagrams contrast an active immune niche (MHCII+ tumor cells and activated T cells) with a resistant niche (MHCII- tumor cells and excluded T cells).
Molecular programs, presented in a mini-heatmap, show differential gene/pathway expression between these niche types.
Direct visual validation includes a bar chart demonstrating a significant difference in patient outcome scores between active and resistant signatures, as well as a validation shield with a checkmark.
The key result concludes that the research identified transcriptional programs driving resistant niches.

R1: List of Supplementary tables
R2: Index | Legend
R3: Table 1 | Description of clinical information in the multidimensional cohort. For seven tumors, there was not matched clinical information.
R5: Table 2 | List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the multidimensional cohort (N=265). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
R7: Table 3 | The median of the median proportions of each immune population per tumor across molecular profiles. First, the median proportion of each immune population was calculated per tumor (across cores); then, for each molecular profile, the median of these tumor-level medians was computed. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis. N of each group is reported in Supplementary Fig. 2a.
R9: Table 4 | Pearson correlation values of the median immune infiltration population across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per population a median immune infiltration was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)

relation of the median infiltration values was calculated across tumors for each pair of immune populations. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251)
R11: Table 5 | False discovery rate (FDR) of the correlation test of the median immune infiltration correlation (Table 3)
R13: Table 6 | Pearson correlation values of the median RCN proportion across the tumors in the multidimensional cohort. Firstly, per RCN a median proportion was calculated per tumor across the cores. Then correlation of the median proportion was calculated across tumors for each pair of RCN. Only tumors with more than one core were used for the analysis (N=251).
R15: Table 7 | False discovery rate (FDR) from the correlation test of the median RCN proportion across tumors (Table 6)
R17: Table 8 | Top predictive markers (from Supplementary Fig. 3c&d) for distinguishing long vs short overall survival (OS) HRDs and HRPs Cancer or Immune-Stromal cells, with reflected across corresponding predicted class median value of min-max scaled expression values after CEFIIRA preprocessing to highlight differences within the cell population. In addition, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.

ion, the number of cells for each comparison are in corresponding columns. P-values were calculated using the Mann-Whitney U test and adjusted using the Benjamini-Hochberg method.
R18: Table 9 | List of overexpressed genes in OVA-TCGA when comparing tumors with high cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=80) compared samples with to low cancer-specific deconvoluted MHCII (HLA_DPB1) expression (n=79) using DESeq2. For each gene is show the mean value, fold change, p.value of test and FDR.
R20: Table 10 | Results of differential expression analysis comparing tumors with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells in t-CycIF (N = 66) to those with a low proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells (N = 67) using the multidimensional cohort. Gene expression was profiled using NanoString technology. The full list of profiled genes (n = 335) is presented. Significance was assessed using a t-test, and p-values were adjusted for multiple testing using the false discovery rate (FDR). Genes were considered differentially expressed based on the following criteria: log2 fold change ≥ 1 or ≤ –1 and FDR < 10⁻³.
R21: Table 11 | Granzyme B response of CD8⁺ T cells in iPDCs following treatment with pembrolizumab (P10, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (T10, 10 µg/mL), or their combination (P10T10).

⁻³.
R21: Table 11 | Granzyme B response of CD8⁺ T cells in iPDCs following treatment with pembrolizumab (P10, 10 µg/mL), tiragolumab (T10, 10 µg/mL), or their combination (P10T10). Responses per sample were calculated as the mean difference between treated (days 2–5) and control (d0) samples and analyzed separately for MHCII-high and MHCII-low cancer sample groups. The mean log2FC across samples in each group is reported in the corresponding column. Statistical significance was assessed using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Averaged changes and pembrolizumab-induced (P10) changes in Granzyme B response are also shown.
R23: Table 12 | List of antibody panel used for t-CycIF in the whole slide validation cohort (N=15). Column description: Cycle=t-CycIF cycle in which used; Antibody=Target of the antibody; Company=Name of provider company; Cat=Catalog number by company; Conc=Concentration used; Purpose=Type of cell of function to identify in experiment.
R25: Table 13 | Logic table to define cell phenotypes using TRIBUS. A value of one (1) is considered a marker expected in the cell phenotype. A value of zero (0) is considered as a not relevant value to define the given cell phenotype. A value of minus one (-1) corresponds to expected negative markers in the corresponding cell phenotype.

**22_NKI_project**

Analysis of a large collection of 265 tumors, using tCycif and 30
panels, and using 10 tissue microarrays, using four cores per tumor.
Further information is in the publication:
[[https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492]{.underline}](https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492)
